title: Your First Program 🚀
description: Write and run your very first line of Python code.

slides.title: Hello, World! 🌍
slides.content:
# Your Very First Program

Every programmer's journey begins with the same tradition — writing a **"Hello, World!"** program. It's like a rite of passage! 🎓

This simple program outputs a message to the screen. In Python, it takes just **one single line** of code.

slides.title: Let's Write It! ✍️
slides.content: Here's your first Python program. It uses the `print()` function to display text on the screen:

slides.title: Breaking It Down 🔍
slides.content:
## What Just Happened?

Let's dissect that one line:

* **`print`** — This is a built-in **function**. Think of it as a command that tells Python: "Display this on the screen!"
* **`(`** and **`)`** — Parentheses wrap around the thing you want to print. They're like hands holding up a sign. 🪧
* **`"Hello, World!"`** — This is a **string** — a piece of text enclosed in quotes. Python needs the quotes to know it's text, not a command.

Put it all together: `print("Hello, World!")` means **"Display the text Hello, World! on the screen."**

quizData.question: What function displays output to the screen in Python?
quizData.explanation: The print() function is Python's built-in way to display output to the screen.

slides.title: Single vs. Double Quotes 🔤
slides.content:
## Both Work!

Python lets you use **single quotes** `'...'` or **double quotes** `"..."` for strings. Both are perfectly valid:

```
print('Hello, World!')
print("Hello, World!")
```

Both produce the exact same output. Pick whichever you prefer — just be **consistent**!

**Rule:** You must start and end with the **same type** of quote. Mixing them like `print('Hello")` will cause an error. ❌

quizData.question: Which of these is a VALID Python string?
quizData.explanation: Strings must start and end with the same type of quote. 'Hello' uses matching single quotes, so it's valid!

slides.title: Try Multiple Lines 🖨️
slides.content: You can use multiple `print()` calls to display several lines of output:
slides.codeSnippet:
print("Hello, World!")
print("My name is Python.")
print("Let's learn together!")

slides.title: Pro Tip 💡
slides.content:
## Python is Case-Sensitive!

In Python, `print` and `Print` are **completely different**. Writing `Print("Hello")` with a capital P will cause an error because Python doesn't recognize `Print` as a built-in function.

Always use **lowercase** `print`. Remember: Python is very picky about capitalization! 🔡

quizData.question: What happens if you write Print('Hello') with a capital P?
quizData.explanation: Python is case-sensitive. 'Print' is not the same as 'print'. Only lowercase print() is the valid built-in function.
